title: Conditional probability and Bayes rule

canonicalExplanation.summary: Bayes' rule updates a prior using likelihood of observed evidence and normalizes across alternatives, making base rates and evidence reliability explicit.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A rare condition with a sensitive test still has a modest positive posterior because false positives among the large unaffected population are included. Your goal is to compute and interpret a posterior probability for a diagnostic scenario. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: AI problems and agents; Problem and environment characteristics. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Bayes' rule updates a prior using likelihood of observed evidence and normalizes across alternatives, making base rates and evidence reliability explicit. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: A posterior is conditional on model assumptions, likelihood is not the posterior, dependent evidence cannot be multiplied as independent, low base rates can dominate, and estimated inputs carry uncertainty. The learner must reject this overclaim: A test that is 95 percent accurate means a positive result gives a 95 percent probability of the condition.

includes: Update belief from prior, likelihood and evidence while avoiding base-rate neglect.
Compute and interpret a posterior probability for a diagnostic scenario.
excludes: A posterior is conditional on model assumptions, likelihood is not the posterior, dependent evidence cannot be multiplied as independent, low base rates can dominate, and estimated inputs carry uncertainty.

outcomes: Compute and interpret a posterior probability for a diagnostic scenario.

examples.title: Conditional probability and Bayes rule: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A rare condition with a sensitive test still has a modest positive posterior because false positives among the large unaffected population are included.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A rare condition with a sensitive test still has a modest positive posterior because false positives among the large unaffected population are included.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Compute the posterior from prior, likelihood, and evidence normalization; sensitivity or headline accuracy alone is not the probability after a positive result.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Compute the posterior from prior, likelihood, and evidence normalization; sensitivity or headline accuracy alone is not the probability after a positive result.

examples.title: Conditional probability and Bayes rule: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: Two correlated symptoms are not multiplied as independent evidence; the model either represents their dependency or reports the simplifying assumption.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: Two correlated symptoms are not multiplied as independent evidence; the model either represents their dependency or reports the simplifying assumption.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: A posterior is conditional on model assumptions, likelihood is not the posterior, dependent evidence cannot be multiplied as independent, low base rates can dominate, and estimated inputs carry uncertainty.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: A test that is 95 percent accurate means a positive result gives a 95 percent probability of the condition.

limitations: encoded facts can be incomplete or inconsistent and probabilistic edges do not automatically establish causation

recap.summary: You should now be able to compute and interpret a posterior probability for a diagnostic scenario. Keep this boundary in view: A posterior is conditional on model assumptions, likelihood is not the posterior, dependent evidence cannot be multiplied as independent, low base rates can dominate, and estimated inputs carry uncertainty.
